title: Authors and Citation
text: Panaro R (2026). spsurv: Bernstein Polynomial Based Semiparametric Survival Analysis. R package version 1.0.2, https://github.com/rvpanaro/spsurv.
code: @Manual{,   title = {spsurv: Bernstein Polynomial Based Semiparametric Survival Analysis},   author = {Renato Panaro},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 1.0.2},   url = {https://github.com/rvpanaro/spsurv}, }

title: Bernstein Polynomial Based Semiparametric Survival Analysis
text: spsurv: R package semi-parametric survival analysis. spsurv package designed contribute flexible set semi-parametric survival regression options, including proportional hazards (PH), proportional odds (PO), accelerated failure time (AFT) models right-censored data. package provides: Survival classes (PH, PO, AFT) extensions based fully likelihood-based approach either Bayesian maximum likelihood (ML) estimation procedures Smooth estimates unknown baseline functions based Bernstein polynomial (BP) Integration Stan user-defined modeling Six distinct prior specification options Bayesian analysis Stan open-source platform language log-probability functions custom likelihoods priors. Access Stan R provided via rstan; package uses NUTS (-U-Turn) sampling default Bayesian fits.

title: Bernstein Polynomial Based Semiparametric Survival Analysis
code: install.packages("spsurv")

title: Bernstein Polynomial Based Semiparametric Survival Analysis
code: install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("rvpanaro/spsurv")

title: Bernstein Polynomial Based Semiparametric Survival Analysis
text: Fit BP-based survival regression PH model: Alternatively, use spbp function: Bayesian analysis approach argument: See reference manual examples.
code: library("KMsurv") data("larynx")  library(spsurv)  fit <- bpph(Surv(time, delta) ~ age + factor(stage), model = "ph", data = larynx) summary(fit) fit <- spbp(Surv(time, delta) ~ age + factor(stage), model = "ph", data = larynx) summary(fit) fit2 <- spbp(Surv(time, delta) ~ age + factor(stage),              approach = "bayes", data = larynx,              iter = 2000, chains = 1, warmup = 1000) summary(fit2)

title: Bernstein Polynomial Based Accelerated Failure Time Model — bpaft
Bernstein Polynomial Based Accelerated Failure Time Model — bpaft
code: library("spsurv") data("veteran", package = "survival") #> Warning: data set ‘veteran’ not found  fit <- bpaft(Surv(time, status) ~ karno + celltype,   data = veteran ) #> Priors are ignored because the MLE approach is used.  summary(fit) #> Bernstein Polynomial based Accelerated Failure Time model #> Call: #> spbp.default(formula = Surv(time, status) ~ karno + celltype,  #>     degree = degree, data = veteran, approach = "mle", model = "aft") #>  #>   n= 137, number of events= 128  #>  #>                       coef exp(coef) se(coef)     z Pr(>|z|)     #> karno              0.03479   1.03540  0.00489  7.12  1.1e-12 *** #> celltypesmallcell -0.74476   0.47485  0.29085 -2.56   0.0104 *   #> celltypeadeno     -0.89813   0.40733  0.28445 -3.16   0.0016 **  #> celltypelarge     -0.13139   0.87687  0.28396 -0.46   0.6436     #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #>                   exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95 #> karno                 1.035      0.966     1.026     1.045 #> celltypesmallcell     0.475      2.106     0.269     0.840 #> celltypeadeno         0.407      2.455     0.233     0.711 #> celltypelarge         0.877      1.140     0.503     1.530 #>  #> Likelihood ratio test= 65.1  on 4 df,   p=2e-13 #> Wald test            = 90.8  on 4 df,   p=<2e-16

Bernstein Polynomial Based Proportional Hazards Model — bpph
code: library("spsurv") data("veteran", package = "survival") #> Warning: data set ‘veteran’ not found  fit <- bpph(Surv(time, status) ~ karno + factor(celltype),   data = veteran ) #> Priors are ignored because the MLE approach is used.  summary(fit) #> Bernstein Polynomial based Proportional Hazards model #> Call: #> spbp.default(formula = Surv(time, status) ~ karno + factor(celltype),  #>     degree = degree, data = veteran, approach = "mle", model = "ph") #>  #>   n= 137, number of events= 128  #>  #>                               coef exp(coef) se(coef)     z Pr(>|z|)     #> karno                     -0.03101   0.96947  0.00516 -6.01  1.9e-09 *** #> factor(celltype)smallcell  0.73446   2.08436  0.25329  2.90  0.00373 **  #> factor(celltype)adeno      1.13852   3.12213  0.29366  3.88  0.00011 *** #> factor(celltype)large      0.32857   1.38898  0.27621  1.19  0.23421     #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #>                           exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95 #> karno                         0.969       1.03     0.960     0.979 #> factor(celltype)smallcell     2.084       0.48     1.269     3.424 #> factor(celltype)adeno         3.122       0.32     1.756     5.552 #> factor(celltype)large         1.389       0.72     0.808     2.387 #>  #> Likelihood ratio test= 59.9  on 4 df,   p=3e-12 #> Wald test            = 61.9  on 4 df,   p=1e-12

title: spbp: The BP Based Semiparametric Survival Analysis Function — spbp.default
code: # Default S3 method spbp(   formula,   degree,   data,   approach = c("mle", "bayes"),   model = c("ph", "po", "aft"),   priors = list(beta = c("normal(0,4)"), gamma = c("lognormal(0,4)"), frailty =     c("gamma(0.01,0.01)")),   cores = min(parallel::detectCores() - 1, 4),   scale = TRUE,   verbose = FALSE,   chains = 4,   ... )

title: spbp: The BP Based Semiparametric Survival Analysis Function — spbp.default
text: formula Surv object time event, status explanatory terms degree Bernstein Polynomial degree data data.frame object approach Bayesian Maximum Likelihood estimation methods, default approach = "bayes" model Proportional Hazards Proportional Odds BP based regression, default model = "ph" priors prior settings Bayesian approach; `normal` `cauchy` beta; `lognormal` `loglogistic` gamma (BP coefficients) cores number core threads use scale logical; indicates whether center scale data verbose verbose passed stan chains number chains passed stan ... arguments passed methods

title: spbp: The BP Based Survival Analysis Function — spbp
spbp: The BP Based Survival Analysis Function — spbp
text: Fits Bernstein Polynomial based Proportional regression survival data.

title: spbp: The BP Based Survival Analysis Function — spbp
code: library("spsurv") data("veteran", package = "survival") #> Warning: data set ‘veteran’ not found  fit_mle <- spbp(Surv(time, status) ~ karno + factor(celltype),   data = veteran, model = "po" ) #> Priors are ignored because the MLE approach is used. summary(fit_mle) #> Bernstein Polynomial based Proportional Odds model #> Call: #> spbp.default(formula = Surv(time, status) ~ karno + factor(celltype),  #>     data = veteran, model = "po", approach = "mle") #>  #>   n= 137, number of events= 128  #>  #>                               coef exp(coef) se(coef)     z Pr(>|z|)     #> karno                     -0.06126   0.94058  0.00877 -6.98  2.9e-12 *** #> factor(celltype)smallcell  1.28840   3.62697  0.43724  2.95   0.0032 **  #> factor(celltype)adeno      1.43779   4.21138  0.47369  3.04   0.0024 **  #> factor(celltype)large      0.10787   1.11390  0.46658  0.23   0.8172     #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #>                           exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95 #> karno                         0.941      1.063     0.925     0.957 #> factor(celltype)smallcell     3.627      0.276     1.539     8.545 #> factor(celltype)adeno         4.211      0.237     1.664    10.657 #> factor(celltype)large         1.114      0.898     0.446     2.780 #>  #> Likelihood ratio test= 73.3  on 4 df,   p=5e-15 #> Wald test            = 65.3  on 4 df,   p=2e-13  fit_bayes <- spbp(Surv(time, status) ~ karno + factor(celltype),   data = veteran, model = "po", approach = "bayes",   cores = 1, iter = 300, chains = 1,   priors = list(     beta = c("normal(0,5)"),     gamma = "halfnormal(0,5)"   ) ) #>  #> SAMPLING FOR MODEL 'spbp' NOW (CHAIN 1). #> Chain 1:  #> Chain 1: Gradient evaluation took 5.6e-05 seconds #> Chain 1: 1000 transitions using 10 leapfrog steps per transition would take 0.56 seconds. #> Chain 1: Adjust your expectations accordingly! #> Chain 1:  #> Chain 1:  #> Chain 1: Iteration:   1 / 300 [  0%]  (Warmup) #> Chain 1: Iteration:  30 / 300 [ 10%]  (Warmup) #> Chain 1: Iteration:  60 / 300 [ 20%]  (Warmup) #> Chain 1: Iteration:  90 / 300 [ 30%]  (Warmup) #> Chain 1: Iteration: 120 / 300 [ 40%]  (Warmup) #> Chain 1: Iteration: 150 / 300 [ 50%]  (Warmup) #> Chain 1: Iteration: 151 / 300 [ 50%]  (Sampling) #> Chain 1: Iteration: 180 / 300 [ 60%]  (Sampling) #> Chain 1: Iteration: 210 / 300 [ 70%]  (Sampling) #> Chain 1: Iteration: 240 / 300 [ 80%]  (Sampling) #> Chain 1: Iteration: 270 / 300 [ 90%]  (Sampling) #> Chain 1: Iteration: 300 / 300 [100%]  (Sampling) #> Chain 1:  #> Chain 1:  Elapsed Time: 0.173 seconds (Warm-up) #> Chain 1:                0.128 seconds (Sampling) #> Chain 1:                0.301 seconds (Total) #> Chain 1:  #> Warning: Bulk Effective Samples Size (ESS) is too low, indicating posterior means and medians may be unreliable. #> Running the chains for more iterations may help. See #> https://mc-stan.org/misc/warnings.html#bulk-ess #> Warning: Tail Effective Samples Size (ESS) is too low, indicating posterior variances and tail quantiles may be unreliable. #> Running the chains for more iterations may help. title: The 'spsurv' package. — spsurv-package
text: set flexible routines allow semiparametric survival regression modeling based Bernstein polynomial, including Bernstein based proportinal hazards model (BPPH), Bernstein polynomial based proportional odds model (BPPO) Bernstein based accelerated failure time model (BPAFT) right-censored data.

title: The 'spsurv' package. — spsurv-package
The 'spsurv' package. — spsurv-package
text: spbp fits semi-parametric models time--event survival data. Non-informative right-censoring assumption available. user-defined Bernstein polynomial can user-defined using arbitrary degree, .e. highest basis polynomials order. framework takes advantage fully likelihood methods since polynomial parameters used estimate baseline functions. Even , said semi-parametric since approach rely distribution. Unlike Cox model, BP based models provide smooth hazard survival curve estimates. _PACKAGE

title: The 'spsurv' package. — spsurv-package
text: Panaro R.V. (2020). spsurv: R package semi-parametric survival analysis. arXiv preprint arXiv:2003.10548. Demarqui, F. N., & Mayrink, V. D. (2019). fully likelihood-based approach model survival data crossing survival curves. arXiv preprint arXiv:1910.02406. Demarqui, F. N., Mayrink, V. D., & Ghosh, S. K. (2019). Unified Semiparametric Approach Model Lifetime Data Crossing Survival Curves. arXiv preprint arXiv:1910.04475. Osman, M., & Ghosh, S. K. (2012). Nonparametric regression models right-censored data using Bernstein polynomials. Computational Statistics & Data Analysis, 56(3), 559-573. Lorentz, G. G. (1953). Bernstein polynomials. American Mathematical Society.

spsurv 1.0.2
text: Handlers: Removed handlers.R; validation setup logic inlined spbp.default. vcov: Block-wise inversion Hessian (regression vs Bernstein polynomial blocks) vcov.spbp. Stan (AFT): clamping u avoid boundary bias; minimum feasible divisor (min_range) range degenerate. MLE: Initial values optimizer drawn prior distributions; retries limited 3 attempts. Examples: data("veteran", package = "survival") docs examples. pkgdown: Reference index updated match current package; Bootstrap 5 / default template; logo size alt-text; README aligned site style; itsamp removed index. Git: .gitignore updated (rj/, .Rcheck, etc.); GitHub Action pkgdown deploy gh-pages.
